speaker: interviewee
text: It very happy that I'm sitting near my wife. And I'd like to introduce you Helene, wonderful wife. I met her in '53 and we got married on December the 19th. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as I told, you all the truth now, in your camera and all my story, I'm telling the truth again. I love that woman very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was inspiration and a big help all my life, since after the war. We have four children which she gave it to me. She was a very good girl. And we have six grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: A seventh on the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a seventh on the way. And I hope to live and be alive and tell my seventh grandchild the story, which is not happy stories. But they got it to learn the story to know what happened to their grandfather. So I wish to stay alive and in the future to tell my seventh grandchild the story as I told you. Thank you, Helene, for all these years listening to my stories and having mercy on me anytime I lose my temper. Because I, and as you know, sometimes still a Dachau ex-prisoner and went through the Holocaust. And you understand it. And you were nice to me. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for a good life.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather, my father's father. And his name is Yankel Rutschaisky. And he was born in Lithuania.

speaker: interviewee
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that picture was taken, I think, in 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lithuania. Yeah, this picture on the left side, this is me. And my name is Moshe Rutschaisky, Rich. Next to me is my mother, Hodel Rutschaisky And her maiden name was [? Flink. ?] On her lap is sitting my sister, Miriam Rutschaisky. Next to my sister is standing my brother, Abraham Rutschaisky. Next to my brother is standing my sister, Faiga Rutschaisky. And in the middle is my father, Chaim Rutschaisky. And that picture was taken when I was five years old, that means about 67 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was taken in Lithuania in Slobodka. This is my sister Faiga. And that, that picture was taken in Italy in Milan in 1948 after the war. She is now 74 years old and she lives in Hollywood, Florida. And her married name is Faiga Salzman. And her husband's name is Mordechai Salzman. This is my sister Miriam. And that picture was taken in 1947 in Germany. She lives now in Israel. And her married name is Fuchs. And her husband's name was Yakov. This is my brother, Abraham. And that picture was taken in 1947 in Germany. And he lives now in Florida, Miami Beach.

speaker: interviewer
text: The name of my wife, and give her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my lovely wife when we got married. Her name is Helene, and her maiden name was [INAUDIBLE]. And now, I made her a Rich, Helene Rich. And I'm very happy that we got married December 19, 1953, Miami Beach. This is me, Morris Rich, before Moshe Rutschaisky. I'm standing by my lathe, by my profession, my trade. And I'm turning wood. And I'm very happy that I had a trade and I could work. And I'm very grateful to my father and grandfather for having a trade. And that saved a little bit staying alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that picture was taken in my shop in Florida at 98 Northwest 29th Street, Miami, in [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] in 1980. OK. From left to right, it's my son Howard Leonard Rich. Next to him is [? Holly ?] Rich, Elliott now. The bride is my daughter, Renee [? Ann ?] Rich. And all the way on the right side is my son, Michael. And that picture was taken two years ago at Renee's wedding, Miami. OK. On the left side is my grandchild Donna. Next to her is my grandson Shawn. Right to Shawn is my grandchild Dora. And on the lower side on the left side, is Brian, my grandson. And all the way on the right side on the lower part, is Gary. And that picture was taken at the wedding of Renee in Miami in 1994. And this is my little grandchild, a sweetheart, Kaitlyn. And this is the daughter of my daughter [? Holly, ?] which her name is Kaitlyn Elliott. That's what she like, we call her. And she's two and a half years old. And that picture was taken right in--

speaker: interviewee
text: School.

speaker: interviewee
text: --school.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Miami?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Miami.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I keep shutting off the bells and I don't know why it's [INAUDIBLE]. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my witness pass when I was a witness in Dachau against the Dachau commandant, Johann Kirsch in 1945. And it was a very happy day in my life [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when that-- when that trial was going on. OK. That, this document is a ausweis. It's like a passport. When I was liberated from Dachau, they gave me that. And it tells on that document my number from the concentration camp, 81840. And it was given out in Munich, Germany, after the liberation. And it was done by the American and the temporary German government.
